summary: The project tackles a classic question—how information is encoded in quantum many‑body states—yet it risks treating entanglement and Kolmogorov complexity as interchangeable without a clear operational bridge. A historical lens shows that the study of entanglement entropy (see Am
body: The most compelling way to understand the proposed link between many‑body entanglement and Kolmogorov complexity is through its lineage in the physics of information. Early work on entanglement entropy (e.g., the 2005 scaling results of Calabrese and Cardy) treated entropy as a proxy for the amount of quantum information shared across a cut, while later computational‑complexity studies (e.g., Brown & Susskind, 2016) introduced circuit‑depth measures as a more tractable stand‑in for algorithmic complexity. Your current formulation jumps directly to "Kolmogorov complexity of the wavefunction" without specifying how it will be approximated for realistic many‑body wavefunctions, which are, by construction, exponentially large. I suggest revising the methodology section to (1) cite the entanglement‑entropy literature as a benchmark, (2) adopt a computable surrogate such as the minimal bond dimension needed for an accurate matrix‑product‑state representation, and (3) explicitly test whether this surrogate correlates with any compression‑based estimates of Kolmogorov complexity. This would ground the project in a well‑established measurement tradition while preserving the exploratory ambition to link physical entanglement with algorithmic information.
